scenarios.name: Specific Performance of Sale Agreement

checks.detail: Specific Relief Act / specific performance cited
At least one statutory section cited
Prayer for execution of sale deed
No invented amounts (10L/50L never in facts)

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT AT BENGALURU
(COMMERCIAL DIVISION)

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

Anita Sharma,
Aged about 52 years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: Indiranagar, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

Rajesh Reddy,
Aged about 60 years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: HSR Layout, Bengaluru.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR SPECIFIC PERFORMANCE OF CONTRACT RELATING TO IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant resides within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court.

COMMERCIAL COURTS ACT COMPLIANCE

4. The Plaintiff states that the present suit is a commercial dispute under Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015. The Plaintiff has complied with the mandatory pre-institution mediation process under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and the same stands concluded/unsuccessful. [Or: The Plaintiff seeks exemption from pre-institution mediation under Section 12A due to urgent interim relief required].

LIMITATION

5. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 54 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from the date fixed for performance, or if no date is fixed, when the plaintiff has notice that performance is refused. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

FACTS OF THE CASE

6. On 10.01.2024, the Plaintiff and the Defendant entered into an Agreement to Sell regarding the residential property bearing Flat No. 302, 3rd Floor, Sunshine Apartments, Koramangala, Bengaluru, measuring 1200 sq ft, for a total consideration of Rs. 85,00,000/-. The said Agreement to Sell is marked as Annexure A.

7. In terms of the said Agreement, the Plaintiff paid an advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- to the Defendant on 10.01.2024 via RTGS bearing UTR No. HDFC20240110987654. The proof of payment is marked as Annexure B.

8. It was agreed between the parties that the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/- was to be paid by the Plaintiff on or before 10.07.2024 against the execution and registration of the Sale Deed.

9. The Plaintiff has always been ready and willing to perform her part of the obligations under the Agreement, including the payment of the balance consideration within the stipulated time.

BREACH OF CONTRACT

10. On 05.06.2024, the Defendant issued a letter to the Plaintiff refusing to execute the Sale Deed, stating that he had received a higher offer for the said property. The said refusal letter is marked as Annexure D.

11. Consequently, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 20.06.2024 calling upon the Defendant to execute the Sale Deed in terms of the Agreement. The legal notice along with the postal receipt is marked as Annexure C.

12. Despite receipt of the said legal notice, the Defendant has failed and refused to execute the Sale Deed or return the advance amount, thereby committing breach of the contract.

DAMAGES

13. By reason of the Defendant's wrongful refusal and breach of contract, the Plaintiff has been kept out of the property and the invested funds, causing financial loss and entitling the Plaintiff to claim costs and interest on the amount paid.

LEGAL BASIS

14. The present cause is governed by Specific Relief Act, 1963, including Section 10 (mandatory after 2018 Amendment — court SHALL grant unless S.11(2), S.14, or S.16 exception), Section 16, Section 21, and Section 22.

15. The present cause is governed by Indian Contract Act, 1872, including Section 10.

CAUSE OF ACTION

16. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 05.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

17. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated {{NOTICE_DATE}}, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

18. The cause of action is continuing in nature until the sale deed is executed or relief is granted.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

19. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Appropriate court fee has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

20. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for specific performance of the Agreement to Sell dated 10.01.2024, directing the Defendant to execute and register the Sale Deed in favour of the Plaintiff upon payment of the balance consideration of Rs. 60,00,000/-;
    (b) In the alternative, if specific performance is not granted, pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for refund of the advance amount of Rs. 25,00,000/- with interest;
    (c) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (d) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (e) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Original agreement dated 10.01.2024

Annexure B — RTGS receipt for Rs. 25,00,000/- (UTR: HDFC20240110987654)

Annexure C — Legal notice dated 20.06.2024 with postal receipt

Annexure D — Defendant's refusal letter dated 05.06.2024

VERIFICATION

Verified at {{CITY}} on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

I, {{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}, the Plaintiff above-named, do hereby verify that the contents of paragraphs 1 to 17 of this plaint are true and correct to my knowledge, and no material facts have been concealed therefrom, and that I have not filed any other suit, petition, or proceeding in any court or before any authority in respect of the same subject matter and cause of action.

DEPONENT

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}}

PLAINTIFF

                                    THROUGH:
                                    {{ADVOCATE_NAME}}
                                    Advocate for the Plaintiff
                                    Enrollment No. {{ENROLLMENT_NO}}
                                    {{ADVOCATE_ADDRESS}}
                                    Mobile: {{ADVOCATE_PHONE}}
                                    Email: {{ADVOCATE_EMAIL}}

scenarios.name: Breach of Contract — Damages (Business)

checks.detail: Total claim Rs.32L or breakdown stated

scenarios.draft_text:
IN THE COURT OF THE Commercial Court (COMMERCIAL DIVISION)
AT Bengaluru

Commercial Suit No. __________ / 20____

TechSoft Solutions Pvt Ltd,
A company registered under the Companies Act,
Having its registered office at: MG Road, Bengaluru.
Represented by its Director: Priya Menon.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

CloudBridge Systems LLP,
A Limited Liability Partnership,
Having its office at: Whitefield, Bengaluru.
Represented by its Designated Partner: Vikram Singh.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR DAMAGES FOR BREACH OF CONTRACT WITH INTEREST AND COSTS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

COMMERCIAL COURT MAINTAINABILITY

1. The subject matter of the present suit arises out of a commercial dispute within the meaning of Section 2(1)(c) of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

2. The suit is valued above the specified value of {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- as prescribed under Section 12 of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015, and is therefore maintainable before this Hon'ble Commercial Court.

JURISDICTION

3. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

4. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the cause of action arose within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Court at Bengaluru, and the Defendant carries on business / resides within such territorial limits.

5. This Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Court. This Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain commercial disputes under the Commercial Courts Act, 2015.

LIMITATION

6. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 55 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of three years from when the contract is broken. The suit is filed well within the prescribed limitation period.

SECTION 12A COMPLIANCE

7. The Plaintiff states that the mandatory pre-institution mediation under Section 12A of the Commercial Courts Act, 2015 has been {{MEDIATION_STATUS_DETAILS}}. [OR: The Plaintiff states that urgent interim relief is being sought, exempting pre-institution mediation under Section 12A(1) proviso.]

FACTS OF THE CASE

8. On 01.02.2024, the Plaintiff entered into a Software Development Agreement with the Defendant for the development of an ERP system, bearing a total contract value of Rs. 45,00,000/- (Annexure A).

9. Pursuant to the terms of the said Agreement, the Plaintiff paid a sum of Rs. 20,00,000/- as the first milestone payment to the Defendant via NEFT on 01.02.2024, bearing UTR Number ICIC20240201456789 (Annexure B).

10. As per the agreed timeline under the Agreement, the Defendant was obligated to deliver the first module of the ERP system on or before 01.05.2024.

11. Upon the Defendant's failure to perform, the Plaintiff issued a legal notice dated 01.07.2024 demanding performance and refund (Annexure E).

BREACH PARTICULARS

12. The Defendant failed to deliver the first module or any part of the ERP system by the stipulated deadline of 01.05.2024, thereby violating the terms contained in {{SPECIFIC_CLAUSES_BREACHED}} of the Agreement.

13. On 15.06.2024, the Defendant expressly abandoned the project without completing the development work, as evidenced by the email correspondence between the parties (Annexure C).

14. Despite the abandonment of the project and the demand made in the legal notice, the Defendant has failed to refund the advance payment of Rs. 20,00,000/- received from the Plaintiff.

DAMAGES

15. The Plaintiff is entitled to the refund of the principal amount of Rs. 20,00,000/- paid as advance, which remains with the Defendant without consideration.

16. Due to the non-delivery of the ERP system and the subsequent delay in operations, the Plaintiff has suffered consequential losses quantified at Rs. 12,00,000/-, assessed as per {{CALCULATION_METHOD_CONSEQUENTIAL}} and supported by the loss assessment report by the auditor (Annexure D).

17. The Plaintiff claims interest at the rate of 18% per annum on the outstanding amount from the date of breach until realisation.

18. Regarding the legal notice dated 01.07.2024, the Defendant's response is as follows: {{LEGAL_NOTICE_RESPONSE}}.

LEGAL BASIS

19. The Defendant has committed a repudiatory breach by refusing to perform its obligations, entitling the Plaintiff to treat the contract as at an end and seek appropriate relief.

20. The Plaintiff is entitled to compensation under Section 73 of the Indian Contract Act, 1872, which provides that when a contract has been broken, the party who suffers by such breach is entitled to receive compensation for any loss or damage caused thereby, which naturally arose in the usual course of things from such breach.

CAUSE OF ACTION

21. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 15.06.2024 when the Defendant committed the breach / wrongful act giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to institute the present suit.

22. The cause of action further arose when the Defendant, despite receipt of the legal notice dated 01.07.2024, failed and refused to comply with the Plaintiff's demand.

23. The cause of action is continuing in nature as the Defendant continues to withhold the rightful dues.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

24. The suit is valued at Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee. Court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint under the provisions of the Court Fees Act, 1870 as applicable in the State of Karnataka. The plaint is properly stamped.

INTEREST

25. The Plaintiff claims interest on the suit amount as follows:

    (a) Pendente lite interest at such rate as this Court deems fit and just from the date of filing of this suit till the date of judgment / decree, under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (b) Future interest at such rate as this Court deems fit and just from the date of judgment / decree till the date of realisation of the entire decretal amount.

PRAYER

In the premises aforesaid, the Plaintiff most respectfully prays that this Court be pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant for a sum of Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/- (Rupees {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE_WORDS}} Only) towards damages;
    (b) Award Rs. {{ACTUAL_LOSS_AMOUNT}}/- towards Actual Loss (Refund of Advance);
    (c) Award Rs. {{CONSEQUENTIAL_LOSS_AMOUNT}}/- towards Consequential loss / damages;
    (d) Award pendente lite and future interest at such rate as this Court deems just under Section 34 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;
    (e) Award costs of the suit including costs of the legal notice;
    (f) Grant such other and further relief(s) as this Court may deem fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

SCHEDULE OF DAMAGES / PARTICULARS OF DAMAGES

The Plaintiff claims the following heads of damages with computation methodology:

26. Actual Loss (Refund)
   Amount claimed: Rs. {{ACTUAL_LOSS_AMOUNT}}/-
   Basis / methodology: Refund of advance payment without consideration
   Documentary proof: NEFT Receipt (Annexure B)

27. Consequential loss / damages
   Amount claimed: Rs. {{CONSEQUENTIAL_LOSS_AMOUNT}}/-
   Basis / methodology: {{CONSEQUENTIAL_LOSS_BASIS}}
   Documentary proof: Loss assessment report (Annexure D)

TOTAL DAMAGES CLAIMED: Rs. {{TOTAL_SUIT_VALUE}}/-

LIST OF DOCUMENTS

Annexure A — Software Development Agreement dated 01.02.2024

Annexure B — NEFT receipt (UTR: ICIC20240201456789) for Rs.20,00,000/-

Annexure C — Email correspondence showing project abandonment

Annexure D — Loss assessment report by auditor

Annexure E — Legal
